elements.sent-2411-003: There has been a growing excitement that implicit graph generative models could be used to design or discover new molecules for medicine or material design.
elements.sent-2411-004: Because these molecules have not been discovered, they naturally lie in unexplored or scarcely supported regions of the distribution of known molecules.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, prior evaluation methods for implicit graph generative models have focused on validating statistics computed from the thick support (e.g., mean and variance of a graph property).
elements.sent-2411-006: Therefore, there is a mismatch between the goal of generating novel graphs and the evaluation methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: To address this evaluation gap, we design a novel evaluation method called \fullname (\name) that systematically creates thin support regions during the train-test splitting procedure and then reweights generated samples so that they can be compared to the held-out test data.
elements.sent-2411-008: This procedure can be seen as a generalization of the standard train-test procedure except that the splits are dependent on sample features.
elements.sent-2411-009: We demonstrate that our method can be used to perform model selection if performance on thin support regions is the desired goal.
elements.sent-2411-010: As a side benefit, we also show that our approach can better detect overfitting as exemplified by memorization.
elements.sent-2411-011: Over the past decade, significant progress has been achieved in enhancing implicit generative models (GANs \citep{goodfellow2014generative , VAEs \citep{kingma2022autoencoding , and diffusion models \citep{sohldickstein2015deep, ho2020denoising ), leading to their extensive use in diverse domains like image and graph generation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In the image generation domain, efforts have been made to standardize evaluation metrics \citep{Wang2004ImageQA, Zhang2018TheUE, NIPS2017_8a1d6947 for comparing the effectiveness of different implicit generative models.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the graph generation domain has yet to adopt a similar standardization.
elements.sent-2411-014: Moreover, while visual inspection of an image can reveal much about its semantic characteristics, this cannot be applied to graphs.
elements.sent-2411-015: Perhaps, more importantly, the application of graph generative models in different areas is quite different than image generators.
elements.sent-2411-016: Instead of aiming to generate an image that looks like others, most graph generative models are designed in hopes that they will be able to generate novel yet interesting graphs, e.g., new molecules with specific properties.
elements.sent-2411-017: While extrapolating far from the known distribution of graphs is indeed challenging, there's potential for generative models to explore novel graphs within underexplored regions of the graph space by leveraging patterns observed in existing graphs.
elements.sent-2411-018: We illustrate this concept and our proposed evaluation methodology in \autoref{fig:shifted-split-illustration , using molecules as an example.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Thick support regions represent known molecules, while thin support regions denote the space of novel graphs.
elements.sent-2411-020: We note that unlike this toy 2D illustration, real graph distributions (like image distributions) are expected to have many areas of thin support in high dimensions though they may be difficult to identify or characterize.
elements.sent-2411-021: Thus, the question arises: How can we measure a graph generative model's ability to generate novel graphs on thin support regions?.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{shifted-split-illustration.png} \caption{ \name systematically thins the distribution in a certain region for training (top row) and then evaluates whether the generated samples in the thinned region after reweighting matches the complementary held-out test dataset (bottom row). In contrast, standard evaluations will seek to match the macro properties (e.g., mean) of this distribution which emphasizes the regions of thick support. The original data (left) illustrates both thick support regions (i.e., areas with many samples) and thin support regions (i.e., areas with very few samples). } \label{fig:shifted-split-illustration}
elements.sent-2411-022: The most intuitive and potentially ideal evaluation approach would involve computing the negative log-likelihood on a test dataset.
elements.sent-2411-023: This metric, relying on the KL divergence, is inherently sensitive to thin support regions.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, for modern implicit generative models, log-likelihood is difficult to compute exactly or even approximate well.
elements.sent-2411-025: Given these challenges, most recent evaluations of generative models seek to compare statistics between generated samples and a held-out test set.
elements.sent-2411-026: A simple approach is to merely compare the means of these distributions or the means of various graph properties.
elements.sent-2411-027: Extending the difference in means to the worst case difference between the expectation of a function is known as Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD).
elements.sent-2411-028: The current and most commonly used standard procedure for evaluating graph generative models is to compute the MMD for the degree, clustering coefficient and orbit count distributions between the generated samples and a held-out set \citep{Niu2020PermutationIG, Chen2021OrderMP, Liao2019EfficientGG, pmlr-v162-hoogeboom22a, Vignac2022DiGressDD .}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, these mean-based approaches focus on the regions of thick support where the most mass is.
elements.sent-2411-030: Thus, they can fail to detect a generative models' performance on the thin support regions---the exact regions where novel graphs could exist.
elements.sent-2411-031: We illustrate this problem in more detail \autoref{sec:appA .}
elements.sent-2411-032: To address this evaluation gap, we focus on matching the statistics (e.g., KS \citep{KolmogorovSmirnov1933SullaDE ) of systematically constructed thin regions of support as illustrated in \autoref{fig:shifted-split-illustration .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Inspired by the classic train-test split idea, we develop a novel method to ``vertically'' split the graph dataset into train and test datasets depending on one graph property.
elements.sent-2411-034: Then, after training, we reweight generated samples and compare them to the corresponding held-out test dataset.
elements.sent-2411-035: At a high level, our evaluation approach, called Vertical Validation (VV), artificially simulates a thin region, but then has ground truth samples from this thinned region to compare against.
elements.sent-2411-036: After reweighting, any metric that can handle weights could be used to compare the generated samples to the held-out samples.
elements.sent-2411-037: We choose the average KS statistic along graph property distributions though other metrics could be used within our framework.
elements.sent-2411-038: This procedure enables the evaluation of the generation capabilities in localized thin support regions rather than focusing on the thick support regions.
elements.sent-2411-039: We summarize our contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-040: We develop a novel ``vertical'' train-test splitting approach that systematically creates thin support in the training data while the testing data has thick support in this region.
elements.sent-2411-041: This can be applied to arbitrary 1D distributions and includes two hyperparameters that control the split sharpness and thickness of full support.
elements.sent-2411-042: We combine this split procedure with a reweighting step to form a novel methodology for evaluating the ability to generate data in thin support regions.
elements.sent-2411-043: We prove that this metric instantiated with the KS statistic is consistent.
elements.sent-2411-044: We empirically validate our \name approach for model selection in the thin support regime of synthetic datasets and then apply \name to compare representative graph generative models on two popular graph datasets.
elements.sent-2411-045: Several methods have been used for evaluating the performance of graph generative models.
elements.sent-2411-046: Some methods can be used for all graph types.
elements.sent-2411-047: These methods include novelty, uniqueness, Wasseretian distance between generated samples and a held-out set, Maximum Mean Dependency (MMD) for the degree, clustering coefficient and orbit count distributions between the generated samples and a held-out set as used by \citet{LiaoGRANs, martinkus2022spectre, Vignac2022DiGressDD, pmlr-v162-hoogeboom22a .}
elements.sent-2411-048: On the other hand, some of the metrics are specific for molecular generation tasks, such as the Frechet ChemNet Distance(FCD) introduced by \citet{Preuer2018FrchetCD , or the Neighbourhood subgraph pairwise distance kernel (NSPDK) MMD introduced by \citet{Costa2010FastNS .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Other metrics include the percentage of atom stability, molecule stability, validity of generated molecules as used by \citet{pmlr-v162-hoogeboom22a, Vignac2022DiGressDD and others.}
elements.sent-2411-050: As one critique of prior evaluations, \citet{o'bray2022evaluation noticed that metrics based on MMD were sensitive to the choice of the kernel functions, the parameters of kernel, and the parameters of the descriptor function.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Despite this progress, there are still deficiencies in the current metrics particularly, when it comes to measuring the ability of the model to generate data in thin support regions.
elements.sent-2411-052: While classic cross validation methods sample form i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-053: Evaluating models under distribution shift has been studied for supervised learning under the names of domain adaptation (DA) \citep{Farahani2020ABR and domain generalization (DG) \citep{Koh2020WILDSAB .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: In both cases, the accuracy metric is evaluated on a test distribution that is different from the training distribution.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, both DA and DG primarily consider supervised learning tasks while we consider generative models.
elements.sent-2411-056: Thus, our approach can be viewed as a type of distribution shift evaluation for generative models.
elements.sent-2411-057: In a similar vein, \cite{bazhenov2023evaluating propose a method for splitting the nodes of a graph into in-distribution and out-of-distribution nodes based on structural properties.}
elements.sent-2411-058: This splitting enables the evaluation of node-level prediction tasks under distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-059: We differ from this work because we split along graphs instead of along nodes, and ours is aimed at evaluating generative models while theirs is focused on node-level tasks.
elements.section-2411-003: Measuring Generalization for Implicit Graph Generative Models
elements.sent-2411-060: Our proposed vertical validation method (VV) can be viewed as a generalized version of cross validation with two main steps.
elements.sent-2411-061: First, we propose a new way to generate biased train-test splits \footnote{We use the term ``split'' here instead of ``fold'' because ``fold'' may seem like uniform splitting.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In particular, our train-test splits thin some regions of the support along the split property.
elements.sent-2411-063: Second, we propose a meta evaluation metric that reweights the generated samples to unbias them and then compares them to the held-out samples using a two-sample metric on other graph properties.
elements.sent-2411-064: This second step is needed because the training sample distribution is different than the held-out test distribution based on our biased train-test splitting.
elements.sent-2411-065: The biased split and reweighting is carefully controlled using only the 1D distributions of the split property.
elements.sent-2411-066: Under these circumstances, if the model can still produce ``good" samples in a region that had thin support, we know that the model can generalize well.
elements.sent-2411-067: Conversely, if the model does not capture the underlying smoothness of the true distribution, it may struggle to generate realistic samples in regions with reduced training data support (underfitting) or it will memorize such data (overfitting).
elements.sent-2411-068: To describe our method, we begin by introducing some notations that we will use for the rest of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-069: Let $p(G)$ denote the true graph distribution of graphs and let $G \sim p(G)$ be a random variable representing a graph, which includes the graph itself and any node or edge attributes if available.
elements.sent-2411-070: Let $m$ be the number user-specified graph properties of interest (e.g., average node degree).
elements.sent-2411-071: These properties are defined by deterministic functions of the graph denoted by $h_\ell: \sG \to \sR$, where $\sG$ is the space of all valid graphs and $\ell \in \{1, \cdots , m\}$.
elements.sent-2411-072: Let ${\bf{h (G): \sG \rightarrow \sR^m$ be the vector function that maps $G$ to its $m$ graph property values, i.e., ${\mathbf{h (G) = (h_1(G), h_2(G), \cdots, h_m(G))$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Furthermore, let $\mathbf{Z = {\bf{h (G)$, where the distribution of $Z$ is the pushforward of the graph distribution under $\bf{h}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Let the true marginal CDFs of each dimension of $\mathbf{Z}$ be denoted by $F_{Z_\ell (Z_\ell)$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: We now define the random vector $\mathbf{U \in [0,1]^m$, where each element is the corresponding CDF value of $Z_\ell$, i.e., $U_\ell = F_{Z_{\ell (Z_\ell) = F_{h_{\ell (G) (h_\ell(G)), \forall \ell \in \{1,2,\cdots,m\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Let $(G_i)_{i=1 ^n$ be our given dataset which are i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-077: For $k$-fold cross validation, we introduce $m$ split variables corresponding to each graph property, denoted as $S_{i,1 , S_{i,2 , \cdots, S_{i,m \in \{1,2, \cdots,k\}$, that indicate the test split for the $i$-th graph using the $\ell$-th split property.}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Let $S_{i,\ell \sim p(S|G_i, h_\ell)$, where $p(S|G_i, h_\ell)$ denotes the splitting distribution which can depend on the graph $G_i$ and the $\ell$-th graph property.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Moving forward, $i$ will be used for the index of the graphs in the sequence $(G_i)_{i=1 ^n$, i.e. $i \in \{1, 2, \cdots n\}$, and $j$ is used for the index of the splits, i.e. $j \in \{1, \cdots , k\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Given these split variables, the held-out dataset from $(G_i)_{i=1 ^n$ of the $j$-th split and $\ell$-th split property will be denoted as $\Xtest^{(\ell,j) = \ldblbrace G_i | \forall i, S_{i,\ell = j\rdblbrace$, where double curly braces $\ldblbrace \rdblbrace$ denotes a multi-set indicating that any element can have a multiplicity more than $1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: The corresponding training dataset will be denoted $\Xtrain^{(\ell,j) = \ldblbrace G_i | \forall i, S_{i,\ell \neq j\rdblbrace$.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: In the first part of our framework, we need to define the distribution $p(S|G_i,h_\ell)$ to create $k$ biased splits for a given graph $G_i$ and $\ell$-th graph property.
elements.sent-2411-084: By biased splits we mean that the split variable depends on the graph, i.e., $P(S_{i,\ell | G_i) \neq P(S_{i,\ell )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: For simplicity, we will assume that the conditional distribution is only dependent on the value of the $\ell$-th graph property, i.e., $p(S|G_i,h_\ell) = p(S|Z_{i,\ell )$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This will enable us to focus on a 1D distribution for splitting and reweighting.
elements.sent-2411-087: Many distributions of $P(S_{i,\ell |G_i)$ could give biased splits, but we wanted both a generic and balanced splitting method, and hence we incorporate the two constraints mentioned below.}
elements.sent-2411-088: For the first constraint, we want our method to work generically for any arbitrary distribution of $Z_{i,\ell}$.
elements.sent-2411-089: Thus, instead of using $Z_{i,\ell}$ directly, we only consider the CDF value of $Z_{i,\ell}$, i.e., we assume $p(S_{i,\ell | G_i, h_\ell)=p(S_{i,\ell | U_{i,\ell )$, where $U_{i,\ell = F_{Z_{i,\ell (Z_{i,\ell ) = F_{h_{\ell (G_i) (h_{\ell (G_i))$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: This means that the splitting only depends on the rank of the $\ell$-th graph property rather than a specific value and thus it can be generically applied to any graph property.
elements.sent-2411-091: Essentially this constraint acts to restrict the space of distributions to those that only depend on $U_{i,\ell}$ making it applicable to any property distribution.
elements.sent-2411-092: For the second constraint, we want the splits to have equal sizes in expectation to ensure that the splits are balanced.
elements.sent-2411-093: To achieve such effect, the marginals of $S$ must be uniform, i.e., we must ensure that $p(S_{i,\ell ) = 1/k$.}

elements.sent-2411-096: In other words this constraint can be simplified to finding a component distribution whose mixture is a uniform distribution and whose weights are equal to $p(S_{i,\ell )$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: One such choice of $p(U_{i,\ell | S_{i,\ell )$ could be disjoint uniform splits corresponding to the quantiles of $U_{i,\ell}$, i.e., $p(U_{i,\ell | S_{i,\ell = j) = p_{\mathrm{Unif [\frac{j-1 {k , \frac{j {k ] (U_{i,\ell )$, where $p_{\mathrm{Unif [a,b] (U) = \frac{1 {b-a}$ denotes a $\mathrm{Uniform}$ distribution between the interval $[a,b]$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-099: To address the issues with the uniform quantile splits, we can resolve to using a different distribution for $p(U_{i,\ell |S_{i,\ell )$ that satisfies the constraint mentioned above.}}

elements.paragraph-2411-005: Using Beta Distributions to Create Smoothed Quantile Splits:
elements.sent-2411-100: For the first issue of unnaturally sharp distribution edges, inspired by empirical Beta copula models \citep{segers2017empirical , we first note that a simple mixture of Beta distributions will have a uniform marginal distribution---the exact property we need for splitting (ie.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Specifically, a mixture of $k$ Beta distributions with parameters defined as: $\alpha_j = j$ and $\beta_j = k + 1 - j$ and for $j \in \{1,2,\cdots, k\}$ will have a uniform distribution \citep{segers2017empirical , i.e., $\sum_j p_{\mathrm{Beta [\alpha_j,\beta_j] (U) = p_{\mathrm{Unif [0,1] (U) $.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Thus we can choose $p(U_{i,\ell |S_{i,\ell ) = p_{\mathrm{Beta [\alpha_j,\beta_j] (U_{i,\ell )$, which will lead to $\sum_{j p(S_{i,\ell = j) p_{\mathrm{Beta [\alpha_j,\beta_j] (U_{i,\ell |S_{i,\ell =j) = p_{\mathrm{Unif [0,1] (U_{i,\ell ) = p(U_{i,\ell )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: However, we still notice some potential issues with this approach, first the intervals have more overlap than what we want, which will be the case if $k$ is relatively small, and second this approach doesn't guarantee a support for all the regions in the current split.
elements.sent-2411-104: To deal with the first concern, we propose adding a sharpness scale ($\sharpness$) that acts to sharpen the edges of the distribution (i.e., making the splits more vertical).
elements.sent-2411-105: The sharpness scale identifies the number of adjacent Beta distributions to mix together for a single split distribution.
elements.sent-2411-106: Specifically, if we use $\sharpness \cdot k$ to be the total number of Beta distributions, then we can let each of the split distributions be mini mixtures of adjacent distributions, i.e., $\pbetamix(U_{i,\ell |S_{i,\ell = j ) = \frac{1 {\sharpness}\sum_{a=1 ^{\sharpness p_{\mathrm{Beta [\alpha_{j,a , \beta_{j,a ] (U_{i,\ell )$, where $\alpha_{j,a = (j - 1)\sharpness + a$ and $\beta_{j,a = \sharpness k + 1 - \alpha_{j,a}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Using this setting will increase the sharpness of the splits, there by decreasing the regional overlap between the adjacent splits.
elements.sent-2411-108: As $\sharpness$ increases we combine more Beta distributions together, and this will lead to a more concentrated and refined edges of the distribution.
elements.sent-2411-109: In the limit as $\sharpness$ goes to infinity, we would recover the quantile splits---thus, this can be seen as relaxation of quantile-based splitting.
elements.sent-2411-110: For the second issue due to zero or near zero support, while our smoothed quantiles can alleviate this somewhat, the support may still be near-zero in certain regions.
elements.sent-2411-111: Thus there will be no samples in training corresponding to the held-out samples.
elements.sent-2411-112: We approach this by mixing our previously defined mixture of the Beta distributions with the uniform distribution as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:dist_choice} p(U_{i,\ell}|S_{i,\ell}) = (1-\epsilon) \pbetamix(U_{i,\ell}|S_{i,\ell}) + \epsilon \cdot p_{\mathrm{Unif}[0,1]}(U_{i,\ell})\end{equation} where $\epsilon \in [0,1]$ is the mixing parameter.
elements.sent-2411-113: This addition will ensure a minimum representation of each region of support in our split, and if $\epsilon=1$, we will get completely random splitting which is similar to standard CV splitting.
elements.sent-2411-114: We summarize our whole splitting procedure in \autoref{fig:scv-illustration , and illustrate the effects of different parameters on our splits in \autoref{fig:explain_all .}}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Step 2: Defining a Meta-metric to Adjust For Shifted Splits
elements.sent-2411-117: The second step in our approach is re-weighting the samples generated by our model to account for initially training the model with a biased dataset.
elements.sent-2411-118: The generative model ($\Omega$) was initially trained with $\Xtrain^{(\ell,j)}$ to produce samples $\Xgen^{(\ell,j)}$ (whose true distribution is $\qGbar$).
elements.sent-2411-119: Those generated samples -or more precisely the properties of such generated samples which we can denote by $\bar{Z}$- will follow a similar distribution to that of the training dataset (assuming that the model doesn't underfit), but will be different from the distribution of the held-out samples $\Xtest^{(\ell,j)}$.

elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Concrete Instantiation of the Metric via KS Statistics
elements.sent-2411-123: As one instantiation for our generic framework, we can choose $\phi$ to be a weighted version of a two-sample KS statistic.
elements.sent-2411-124: One of the reasons for choosing the KS statistic is that its values are always between 0 and 1, and its weighted version can handle weighted samples from two distributions.
elements.sent-2411-125: Given any two weighted graph datasets $\cG_{1,W_1}$ and $\cG_{2,W_2}$.
elements.sent-2411-126: The empirical weighted KS statistic of a specific graph property function $h(\cdot)$ with $Z_{i = h(G_i)$ can be defined as follows: \begin{equation}\phi_{KS}(\cG_{1,W_1}, \cG_{2,W_2}; h) = \sup_{G_i} |\hat{F}_{W1}(h(G_i)) - \hat{F}_{W2}(h(G_i)) | \notag \quad= \sup_{Z_{i}} |\hat{F}_{W1}(Z_{i}) - \hat{F}_{W2}(Z_{i})|, \label{eq:phi_KS}\end{equation} where $\hat{F _{W1 (z) = \frac{1 {\sum_i W_i \sum_i W_i {\bf 1 _{(Z_{i \leq z)}$ is the weighted empirical CDF associated with $\cG_{1,W_1}$ and similarly for $\hat{F _{W2 (z)$ associated with $\cG_{2,W_2}$.}}}}}}}}}

elements.paragraph-2411-010: Generating Molecules on Thin Support Regions
elements.sent-2411-214: In practice, novel molecule generation may focus on generating molecules within the thick support (rather than thin support) of the marginal property distributions because those molecules would be most similar to known molecules.
elements.sent-2411-215: However, we argue that evaluating generation on thin support is still important because thin support regions in the joint distribution could be hidden in the thick support of marginal distributions, especially when considering a high-dimensional distributions.
elements.sent-2411-216: For example, consider samples on a 3D sphere.
elements.sent-2411-217: When projected onto any of the three dimensions, it will look like the support is dense near zero.
elements.sent-2411-218: However, the distribution has no support at or near the all zero vector.
elements.sent-2411-219: Thus, we hypothesize that in high dimensional spaces, there are many thin support regions that are hidden.
elements.sent-2411-220: When we systematically create thin support regions using our approach, the goal is to measure the model's ability to generalize to thin support in general (including thin support of the joint distribution).
elements.sent-2411-221: Thus, while in practice novel molecule generation may focus on generating molecules with the thick support of the marginal property distributions, we test the ability of the model to generate in those regions as this will reflect its ability to generate in thin support of the joint distribution.
elements.sent-2411-222: Our approach when used exhaustively as presented in the experiments of \autoref{sec:scv can be computationally burdensome, however practically we would choose only a single split feature and a single split index as we did in \autoref{sec:vtrain-val-vtest and this would avoid the added computational cost.}}
elements.sent-2411-223: While we recommend choosing a split feature that is maximally dependent on other features as we discuss in \autoref{sec:add_exp , choosing the split property is still an area of potential optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Additionally, because our method depends heavily on the joint distribution of the chosen properties, we recommend that the user pre-examine the property distributions and carefully select relevant properties, where properties with smooth distributions will likely be better for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-225: Also, our method is limited to 1-dimensional properties.
elements.sent-2411-226: Generalizing our method to multivariate splits is an area for future work.
elements.sent-2411-227: Finally, we note that estimating sample weights is complex and while our kernel mean matching (KMM) approach worked reasonably well in our case, choosing the kernel parameters or using more advanced weight estimation approaches is an open area of exploration (more discussion in \autoref{sec:implementation-other ).}
elements.sent-2411-228: Therefore, we hope our work opens up new avenues of research.
elements.sent-2411-229: In summary, we introduced \fullname, a new framework for biased splitting and reweighting, to evaluate the generalizability of implicit graph generative models on thin support regions.
elements.sent-2411-230: We developed a practical algorithm to perform this given a set of graph properties.
elements.sent-2411-231: We demonstrated that this validation approach can be used to select models which will generalize better to thin support regions.
elements.sent-2411-232: Ultimately, we hope that our approach is a step in establishing more concrete and robust evaluation methodologies for graph generative models.

elements.sent-2411-238: In summary, prior evaluation approaches for implicit generative models are limited in their ability to measure performance on thin support regions.

elements.subsection-2411-005: A Generalization of the Empirical CDF
elements.sent-2411-245: To project samples onto the unit interval, we use a generalization of the empirical CDF (ECDF) where the test points may be different from the training points and where ties (e.g., in discrete spaces) can be handled appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-246: The core idea is that for test points we want to project, denoted by $\mathcal{A}$, we find the nearest point in the base dataset ($\mathcal{B}$) and evenly spread out the projections corresponding to the ECDF interval.
elements.sent-2411-247: We begin with an illustration to explain this in both continuous and discrete cases in \Cref{fig:cont_ecdf and \Cref{fig:discrete_ecdf respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-248: To define it formally, we first begin with a simpler randomized uniform projection method and then develop the non-randomized version that yields a deterministic low discrepancy sequence, also known as a centered regular lattice \citep[Chapter 1]{dick2010digital .}
elements.figure-2411-016: [H] \includegraphics[width=.48\textwidth]{ecdf_cont.pdf} \includegraphics[width=.5\textwidth]{test_samples_cont.pdf} \caption{On the left in orange are continuous valued base samples of dataset $\mathcal{B}$ after being projected onto the uniform space using the ECDF. On the right in green are the continuous valued test samples of dataset $\mathcal{A}$ after being projected onto the uniform space using the nearest point in the base dataset $\mathcal{B}$ } \label{fig:cont_ecdf}
elements.figure-2411-017: [H] \includegraphics[width=.45\textwidth]{ecdf_discrete.pdf} \includegraphics[width=.45\textwidth]{test_samples_discrete.pdf} \caption{On the left in orange are discrete valued base samples of dataset $\mathcal{B}$ after being projected onto the uniform space using the ECDF and breaking ties between the same points at random. On the right in green are the discrete valued test samples of dataset $\mathcal{A}$ after being projected onto the uniform space using the nearest point in the base dataset $\mathcal{B}$. Note that if the test samples are outside the original data (as the two test points at 3.0), they will be mapped to the nearest base point (2.0 in this figure) and then projected evenly onto the interval spanning the corresponding part of the ECDF. } \label{fig:discrete_ecdf}
elements.sent-2411-249: First, let us define the left and right empirical CDFs (the standard ECDF is the right empirical CDF).
elements.sent-2411-250: Informally, these will give us the bottom and top of each ``step'' in the empirical CDF.
elements.sent-2411-251: Formally, given a 1D dataset $\mathcal{C}$ as: \begin{equation}\hat{F}_{C}^{(-)}(a) = \frac{1}{|C|}\sum_{c_i \in C} 1(c_i < a) \hat{F}_{C}^{(+)}(a) = \frac{1}{|C|} \sum_{c_i \in C} 1(c_i \leq a) \,,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{1 (\cdot)$ is an indicator function that is 1 if the condition is true and 0 otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-252: Note that the only difference between these is that in the left ECDF $\hat{F _{\mathcal{C ^{(-)}$, the condition does not include equality.}}}
elements.paragraph-2411-014: Na\"ive Randomized Projection to Unit Interval.
elements.sent-2411-253: First, we will consider a randomized projection to the uniform based on the left and right ECDFs defined above.
elements.sent-2411-254: Formally, with two scalar datasets, $\mathcal{A = \{ a_i \in \R \}_{i=1 ^{|\mathcal{A |}$ and a base dataset $\mathcal{B = \{ b_i \in \R \}_{i=1 ^{|\mathcal{B |}$, we define our function as: \begin{equation}F_{rand}(A ; B) \triangleq \{(1-V_i) \hat{F}_{B}^{(-)}(b^*(a_i)) + V_i \hat{F}_{B}^{(+)}(b^*(a_i)) : a_i \in A \} \,,\end{equation} where $b^*(a_i) := \argmin_{b \in \mathcal{B \|a_i - b\|_2^2$ represents the closest point in $\mathcal{B}$ to $a_i$ and $V_i \sim \text{Uniform (0,1)$ is an independent uniform random variable corresponding to each value $a_i \in \mathcal{A}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: This projects each point $a_i$ uniformly over the interval $[\hat{F _{\mathcal{B ^{(-) (b^*(a_i)), \hat{F _{\mathcal{B ^{(+) (b^*(a_i))]$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-256: In the special case where $\mathcal{A = \mathcal{B}$, i.e., the test points are the same as the base dataset, then this produces a marginally uniform distribution over $[0,1]$ because each test point is randomly projected on the $1/|\mathcal{B |$ interval of the ECDF corresponding to itself.}}
elements.sent-2411-257: Even if there are ties in the dataset (e.g., with a discrete dataset), this will still produce a uniform marginal distribution because the ECDF will jump by the $n_{\text{ties /|\mathcal{B |$, where $n_{\text{ties }$ is the number of ties for a particular value in $\mathcal{B}$.}}}}
elements.paragraph-2411-015: Low Discrepancy Projection to Unit Interval.
elements.sent-2411-258: The main drawback to the projection method described above is that it is random and may not evenly space out points across the unit interval [0,1], i.e., the sequence may not have low discrepancy compared to evenly spaced points \citep{dick2010digital .}
elements.sent-2411-259: Therefore, we propose a non-randomized version of the above projection method that evenly spaces test points across the corresponding interval instead of choosing a random point in the interval, i.e., we replace $V_i$ with a deterministic function.
elements.sent-2411-260: This form of spacing is known as a regular centered lattice \citep[Chapter 1]{dick2010digital .}
elements.sent-2411-261: Formally, we define our non-randomized function as: \begin{equation}F(A ; B) := \{(1-v(a_i)) \hat{F}_{B}^{(-)}(b^*(a_i)) + v(a_i) \hat{F}_{B}^{(+)}(b^*(a_i)) : a_i \in A \} \,,\end{equation} where $b^*(a_i)$ is the closest point as defined in the randomized version but we replace a random $V_i$ with a determinstic function of the sets defined as follows: \begin{equation}v(a_i) := \frac{\textnormal{sorted\_index}(a_i, A(a_i)) - 0.5}{|A(a_i)|} A(a_i) := \{ a_j \in A: b^*(a_j) = b^*(a_i) \} \,.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}v(a_i) := \frac{\textnormal{sorted\_index}(a_i, A(a_i)) - 0.5}{|A(a_i)|} A(a_i) := \{ a_j \in A: b^*(a_j) = b^*(a_i) \} \,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-262: Note that $v(a_i) \in [0,1]$ by construction and it evenly spaces the points in the equivalence class of $\mathcal{A (a_i)$ over the interval corresponding to the equivalence class.}
elements.sent-2411-263: For example, if $a_i=1$ and $\mathcal{A (a_i) = \{1,0.5,1.5\}$, then $v(a_i) = \frac{\textnormal{index (a_i,\mathcal{A (a_i)) -0.5 {|\mathcal{A (a_i)| = \frac{2-0.5 {3 = \frac{1.5 {3 = 0.5$, i.e., it would project this point to the center of the interval $[\hat{F _{\mathcal{B ^{(-) (b^*(a_i)), \hat{F _{\mathcal{B ^{(+) (b^*(a_i))]$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-311: For the experiments in \autoref{sec:vtrain-val-vtest , we incorporated an early stopping criterion.}

elements.sent-2411-317: In \autoref{sec:exp_val we chose a single split property out of 3 possible properties, and in \autoref{sec:vtrain-val-vtest we chose one out of 5 possible properties.}}
elements.sent-2411-318: Intuitively, a split property which is completely independent of the other test properties would not cause any (indirect) shift of the other properties.
elements.sent-2411-319: Thus, the marginal distributions of each property would be equal for every split.
elements.sent-2411-320: This would not enable good evaluation of the generalization performance on the thin support since a model could just copy the marginal distributions of the properties from the training split.
elements.sent-2411-321: Therefore, we choose to find a split property that has high dependence with all other split properties.
elements.sent-2411-322: Concretely, in both cases we calculated the spearman correlation between all the features against each other and computed the average absolute correlation that one feature has with the rest, then choose the feature that has the highest average correlation to be the split feature.
elements.sent-2411-323: As we have mentioned in the discussion, this is still an area of exploration, but the motivation behind this approach was that choosing a split feature that is somewhat correlated with the others will cause the distribution of the other features to change accordingly which is the desirable effect in our case.
